translatedText: Composed the opera 'The Village Soothsayer'.
Immersed himself in political and social institutions for the Academy of Dijon contest, laying the groundwork for 'The Social Contract'.
Settled in L'Ermitage and began writing 'The Social Contract', 'Julie, or the New Heloise', and 'Emile'.
Had a disagreement with Diderot over the social function of theater and broke off their friendship a year later.
'Julie, or the New Heloise' was published in Paris and achieved great success.
Published 'Emile' and 'The Social Contract', facing persecution due to them and fleeing to Switzerland.
Began writing 'Confessions' after being attacked by Voltaire's anonymous pamphlet 'The Sentiment of the Citizens'.
Completed 'Confessions'.
Returned to Paris.
Wrote 'Rousseau Judge of Jean-Jacques'.
Began writing 'Reveries of the Solitary Walker' but was unable to finish it.
Died on July 2.
The Social Contract
A book that perfected the theory of popular sovereignty and established the general will as a condition for the legitimate exercise of power.
Discourse on the Arts and Sciences
The first-prize-winning work in the French Academy contest, containing thoughts that go beyond the limits of the Enlightenment.
Discourse on the Origin of Inequality
A book that explains the inequality arising from the institution of property rights and the development of social organization in contrast to the state of nature.
Emile
Described the theory of natural religion, greatly influencing 18th-century thought but leading to his persecution.
Participation in compiling the Encyclopédie
Participated in the compilation of Diderot and d'Alembert's 'Encyclopédie', writing articles on music and political economy.
Theory of the General Will
Presented the concept of the will of human nature for a form of association that pursues the common good and equal freedom.
Thorough egalitarian thought
Delved into the issue of human equality practically for the first time in thought history, asserting that economic inequality is the root of all social evils.
Who is Jean-Jacques Rousseau?
Jean-Jacques Rousseau was a French social contract theorist, direct democrat, republican, and Enlightenment philosopher born in the Republic of Geneva, Switzerland.
What does Rousseau's 'general will' mean?

Rousseau's general will is the will to pursue a form of association in which the whole common force defends and protects the person and goods of each associate, and in which each, while uniting himself with all, may still obey himself alone, and remain as free as before.
Why did Rousseau argue to 'return to nature'?

Rousseau did not reject civilization itself, but strongly argued for criticizing the absurdity and contradictions of a civilized society lacking freedom and equality, and restoring the archetype of a society where humans' innate natural rights to freedom and equality are guaranteed.
What are Rousseau's major works?
Rousseau's major works include 'The Social Contract', 'Discourse on the Arts and Sciences', 'Discourse on the Origin of Inequality', 'Emile', and 'Julie, or the New Heloise'.
Born in Freiberg in Mähren, Moravia, Austro-Hungarian Empire.
Acknowledged the lack of results in his research on eel reproductive organs in his first paper, 'The Testes of the Eel'.
Received his medical doctorate with a thesis 'On the Spinal Cord of Lower Fish Species'.
Around October 1885
Stayed in Paris for collaborative research with Jean-Martin Charcot.
Married Martha Bernays and opened his own neurology clinic.
Published 'Studies on Hysteria' co-authored with Josef Breuer.
November 1899
Published 'The Interpretation of Dreams'.
First noticed abnormalities in his oral cavity.
A tumor was first discovered in his mouth.
Awarded the Goethe Prize for his contributions to psychology and German literature.
As the Nazis took power in Germany, many of his books were burned.
June 1938
Fled to London, England after the Nazi German annexation of Austria.
September 23, 1939
Died.
Founder of Psychoanalysis
As an Austrian psychologist, he founded the field of psychoanalysis.
Theory of the unconscious and repression
Created theories on the unconscious and the defense mechanism of repression.
Psychoanalytic clinical therapy
Created a clinical treatment method to treat psychopathology through dialogue between the patient and the psychoanalyst.
Redefinition of sexuality
Redefined sexual desire as a primary motivational energy in human life.
Theory of Transference
Invented the theory of emotional transference within the therapeutic relationship.
Dream interpretation technique
Invented a technique for observing 'unconscious desires' through dreams.
Concept of mental structure
Stated that the mind consists of the conscious, preconscious, and unconscious, and proposed the concepts of id, ego, and superego.
Instinct theory
Proposed the life instinct (Libido) and the death drive (Thanatos).
Who is Sigmund Freud?
Sigmund Freud was an Austrian psychologist and the founder of psychoanalysis.
What are the main theories created by Freud?
He created theories on the unconscious and the defense mechanism of repression, psychoanalytic clinical therapy, and the redefinition of sexual desire as a primary motivational energy.
How did Freud explain the structure of the mind?
Freud stated that the mind is composed of the conscious, preconscious, and unconscious, and proposed the concepts of the id, ego, and superego.
When did Freud die?
Sigmund Freud died on September 23, 1939.
Born in Switzerland.
His father, Paul Jung, was appointed to a larger parish in Laufen (when Jung was 6 years old).
March 3, 1907
Met Sigmund Freud for the first time in Vienna.
Around 1913
Started working independently on his analytical psychology theories.
Resigned from the Psychoanalytic Association.
Until resigning in 1914
Served as the president of the International Psychoanalytical Association.
Published the book 'Psychology of the Unconscious'.
Published 'A Psychological Approach to the Dogma of the Trinity'.
Published 'Transformation Symbolism in the Mass'.
Published 'Answer to Job'.
June 6, 1961
Died at the age of 85.
His book 'Memories, Dreams, Reflections' was published posthumously.
Founder of Analytical Psychology
As a Swiss psychiatrist, he charted his own course, pioneering and founding the field of analytical psychology.
Establishment of the Complex concept
Used the term 'complex' to describe a specific psychological state and laid the foundation for theories related to it.
Establishment of the Collective Unconscious concept
Established the concept of the collective unconscious, a reservoir of psychic material shared through human history and culture, separate from personal experience.
Personality classification (Introvert/Extravert)
Classified personalities into introverted and extraverted types.
Development of the Individuation concept
Developed and theorized psychotherapy, believing that individuals undergoing therapy could evolve into whole personalities through the process of 'individuation'.
Advocacy of the Self archetype
Argued that the Self archetype possesses a transcendent function that enables the inner unconscious to realize a unified whole.
Concepts of Anima and Animus
Presented the concepts of Anima and Animus, representing the opposite sex within a person's psyche.
Concept of Archetypes
Borrowed from anthropology, he established the concept of archetypes, which denote universal psychic images or themes.
Concept of Persona
Presented the concept of the Persona, a facet of personality that emerges for adaptation or personal convenience.
Concept of the Shadow
Presented the concept of the Shadow, encompassing repressed and unacknowledged aspects of personality, often regarded as negative.
Concept of the Self
The central concept governing the individuation process; he viewed the 'Self', symbolized by the mandala unifying feminine and masculine, as the primary archetype of the psyche.
Who is Carl Jung?

Carl Jung was a Swiss psychiatrist and the founder of analytical psychology, who established the concepts of the complex and collective unconscious, and classified personality into introverted and extraverted types.
Why did Carl Jung and Freud part ways?
While Freud limited the concept of 'libido' to the sexual, Jung viewed it as a broader, more general psychic energy, leading to their parting due to this difference in perspective.
What field of psychology did Carl Jung found?
Taking an independent path, Carl Jung pioneered and founded the field of analytical psychology.
What did Carl Jung say about God?
In a television interview, when asked about God, he replied, "I don't need to believe. I know."
